title: Classification of Systems
summary: This page discusses causal and noncausal (anticipative) systems, explaining that any real-time practical system must be causal since it cannot respond to future inputs. An example system y(t) = x(t-2) + x(t+2) is used to illustrate noncausal behavior, with accompanying figures showing input-output relationships. Example 1.13 begins, asking students to assess causality of three given systems.

figures.caption: Input–output of a noncausal system and the causal output achieved by delay.
figures.description: The figure has three time-domain plots with horizontal axis t and vertical labels x(t), y(t), and ŷ(t). In (a), the input x(t) is a unit-height rectangular pulse from t=0 to t=1; in (b), the output y(t) consists of two unit pulses from t=-2 to -1 and from t=2 to 3, showing one response occurs before the input and is therefore noncausal. In (c), the delayed output ŷ(t) shifts the pulses to t=0 to 1 and t=4 to 5, illustrating how adding delay makes the output causal. Students should notice the timing relationship between input and output and how delay removes the advance response.

figures.caption: Example 1.13 asks students to assess the causality of three continuous-time systems: y(t)=x(−t), y(t)=x(t+1), and y(t+1)=x(t).
figures.description: A boxed textbook example titled "Example 1.13 Assessing System Causality" presents three continuous-time system equations to evaluate: (a) y(t)=x(-t), (b) y(t)=x(t+1), and (c) y(t+1)=x(t). There are no plotted axes; the figure is a text panel with labeled parts (a), (b), and (c), plus a footnote stating that in real-time operation the response is essentially simultaneous with the input. Students should notice the time reversal and time shifts in the input-output relationships and relate them to whether the output depends on present, past, or future input values.
